hook: A mail carrier's hand-built folk-art maze devoted to his favourite fruit, the orange.
facts: Built single-handedly by retired postal worker Jeff McKissack at 2401 Munger St in Houston's East End between 1956 and his death in 1979.
A 3,000-square-foot maze of arenas, balconies, walkways and exhibits assembled from salvaged tractor seats, tiles, wagon wheels and figurines, dedicated to the health virtues of the orange.
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places on 21 November 2006 (reference 06001063).
It is the namesake site of the nonprofit Orange Show Center for Visionary Art, founded in 1980, which also operates the Beer Can House and Smither Park.

Seasonal hours: open weekends noon-5pm in fall/spring and Wednesday-Sunday in summer; admission has been about $5; the monument has periodically closed for restoration under a Save America's Treasures grant, viewable from the street when closed.
w: For more than two decades a Houston mailman named Jeff McKissack hand-built this dazzling maze of mosaics, wheels, flags and found objects — a folk-art temple to the humble orange and the virtues of hard work and clean living. He died in 1980, weeks after it opened. Climb its little stages and balconies. One man's monument to a fruit.

hook: A bungalow sheathed in an estimated 50,000 flattened beer cans, with shimmering can-tab curtains.
facts: Created by retired Southern Pacific Railroad upholsterer John Milkovisch at 222 Malone St in the Rice Military neighbourhood, who began the project in 1968 and continued through the late 1980s.
The house and yard are clad in an estimated 50,000-plus flattened beer cans, plus bottle caps, marbles and beer-can-tab garlands that hang as curtains.
It is owned and operated as a museum by the Orange Show Center for Visionary Art.
In January 2025 the Beer Can House received national preservation recognition.
It is open for limited public hours and tours rather than continuously; the exterior can be viewed from the street.
w: Tired of mowing the lawn, a railroad upholsterer named John Milkovisch began paving his yard with marbles and concrete in 1968 — then siding his whole house in flattened beer cans, some 50,000 of them. Garlands of cut cans still sing in the wind. He drank every one himself, he claimed. A monument to thrift, beer and not gardening.

hook: A half-acre community mosaic park next to the Orange Show, with a 400-foot 'Memory Wall'.
facts: Located at 2441 Munger St in the historic East End, immediately adjacent to the Orange Show monument and operated by the Orange Show Center for Visionary Art.
Designed by visionary builder Dan Phillips with the late Stephanie Smither in memory of her husband, collector and Orange Show board member John H. Smither.
More than 300 artists have contributed permanent mosaic work made from recycled and found materials such as tile, shells, keys, dishes and discarded trophies.
Features include the 400-foot Memory Wall (60 panels), the Lindley Fish Amphitheater, the Vinson & Elkins Pavilion, mosaic swings, a meditation garden and a kinetic-sculpture tower by Smitty Regula.
Free and open to the public daily from dawn until dusk.
w: Right beside the Orange Show, more than 300 folk artists have studded a long undulating wall with mosaic, mirror and broken china into one collaborative dreamscape. A memory wall and swinging stage make Smither Park a monument to community outsider art. Free, and forever unfinished. Houston's communal craft, set in grout.

hook: A line of giant welded-metal beasts guarding a pipe yard along Highway 288.
facts: An open-air display of monumental metal sculptures along the edge of the Texas Pipe & Supply Company's pipe yard (offices at 2330 Holmes Rd / W. Bellfort Ave), just south of Houston's 610 Loop beside State Highway 288.
The collection grew from a concrete hippo placed in 1987 by Texas Pipe chairman Jerry Rubenstein and now includes more than 20 oversized works.
Sculptures, many built from the company's surplus pipe and equipment, were created chiefly by artist Ron Lee, with contributions from light-metals artist Mark Rankin.
Pieces include a towering daddy-long-legs spider, a King Kong hanging from a crane, and a massive armadillo; no new works have been added since Ron Lee's death in 2017.
The sculptures sit on private property and are intended to be viewed from the highway frontage road rather than entered.
w: Along the frontage of a working 108-acre pipe yard march a parade of huge welded creatures — a praying mantis, a giant ant, dragons and dinosaurs, welded by the company's own metalworkers. You view them from the highway as you pass, a surreal jolt in the sprawl. No tickets, just a slow drive-by. Monsters made of pipe.

hook: Four 18-foot presidential and Texan busts looming over the I-10/I-45 interchange.
facts: Located at 1400 Elder St near the Interstate 10 / Interstate 45 interchange in Houston; at roughly a third of an acre it is described as the smallest park in Harris County.
Holds four 18-foot, roughly two-ton busts depicting George Washington, Abraham Lincoln, Stephen F. Austin and Sam Houston.
The busts were created by Houston sculptor David Adickes (made around 2008) and donated to Harris County in November 2012.
The Houston Chronicle has estimated about three million vehicles pass and can see the busts each day.
Operated by Harris County Precinct 1; grounds open to the public, with posted weekday hours around 7am-6pm and no admission charge.
w: Four colossal heads loom over a scruffy lot by the freeways: eighteen-foot busts of Washington, Lincoln, Stephen F. Austin and Sam Houston, cast by local sculptor David Adickes. Nicknamed 'Mount Rush Hour,' they stare out over the traffic with stony patriotism. Pull over and crane up. Giant statesmen, marooned in the sprawl.

hook: A block of restored 1930s shotgun houses turned into a socially engaged art project.
facts: Founded in 1993 by artist Rick Lowe with six other Houston artists (James Bettison, Bert Long Jr., Jesse Lott, Floyd Newsum, Bert Samples and George Smith) in the historic Third Ward.
The original holdings comprised 22 shotgun houses, built in the 1930s, on a block and a half at the corner of Holman and Live Oak streets (campus address around 2521 Holman St).
Houses serve as artist residencies and exhibition spaces, often focused on African-American themes, alongside programmes that have provided transitional housing for young single mothers.
The organisation has expanded well beyond the original 22 structures to dozens of properties across the surrounding neighbourhood.
Founder Rick Lowe was awarded a MacArthur Fellowship in 2014 for the project's blend of art and community development.
w: In 1993 seven Black artists rescued a row of derelict shotgun houses in the Third Ward and turned them into a living laboratory of socially engaged art, residencies and community uplift, co-founded by Rick Lowe. The wooden houses now hold rotating installations, not blight. A row that rewrote what a gallery could be.

hook: Former rice-storage silos reborn as artist studios and a honeycomb 'cave' gallery.
facts: Located at 1502 Sawyer St in Houston's historic First Ward, in a repurposed former rice-storage facility.
The complex provides about 97 workspaces for over 100 artists, plus retail, gallery and office space for creative entrepreneurs.
It houses SITE Gallery Houston, set inside 34 intersecting round concrete grain chambers that form a roughly 9,000-square-foot honeycomb footprint.
The Silos anchor the wider Sawyer Yards creative district, a roughly 55-acre former-industrial campus that is home to several hundred artists and studios.
Studios and galleries are open to the public during events and posted hours, including periodic open-studio days.
w: A row of fat concrete rice silos, once part of a riverside grain operation, now holds artists' studios and a gallery in the round chambers. Part of the Sawyer Yards complex, the silos open on art-walk days. Industrial Houston, hollowed out and handed to painters. Climb inside a grain tube turned art space.

hook: A cathedral-like 1926 underground water reservoir with a 17-second echo, reopened as an eerie subterranean art space.
facts: Built in 1926 as a City of Houston drinking-water reservoir, designed to hold about 15 million gallons across an 87,500-square-foot underground chamber.
Decommissioned in 2007 after an irreparable leak was discovered.
Restored by Buffalo Bayou Partnership and opened to the public for tours and art installations in May 2016.
Its 221 concrete columns and water-tight basin produce a reverberation of roughly 17 seconds.
Located within Buffalo Bayou Park on the western edge of downtown Houston, accessed near Sabine Street and Memorial Drive (105B Sabine St).
w: Beneath a city park hides a vast 1926 drinking-water reservoir, an underground hall of 221 columns reflected in a skin of water, with a seventeen-second echo. Decommissioned after a leak, it reopened for tours and art in 2016. Whisper, and the dark answers slowly. Houston's accidental subterranean cathedral.

hook: A science museum where you walk through a giant human body, including the crawl-through Colossal Colon.
facts: Officially the John P. McGovern Museum of Health and Medical Science, located at 1515 Hermann Drive in Houston's Museum District near Hermann Park.
Its signature Amazing Body Gallery is a larger-than-life walk-through of the human body.
The museum has displayed the 'Colossal Colon,' a giant walk-through colon model used to raise colorectal-cancer awareness.
It is a member institution of the Houston Museum District.
w: A science museum where you can walk through a giant intestine: the Health Museum's crawl-through colon, polyps and all, sits among oversized body parts and hands-on biology. Gleefully gross and genuinely educational, aimed at curious kids. Stroll the digestive tract and learn how your heart works. Anatomy, supersized.

hook: A long-running Tolkien-themed cafe stuffed with Lord of the Rings memorabilia and hobbit-sized sandwiches.
facts: Located at 2243 Richmond Avenue, Houston, TX 77098, in the Greenway / Upper Kirby area.
Established in 1972; the menu and decor are themed around J.R.R. Tolkien's hobbit tradition.
Confirmed open in 2026, serving burgers, oversized sandwiches, seafood and weekend brunch.
The dining rooms are filled with Lord of the Rings memorabilia, giving the restaurant its name.
w: A leafy, ramshackle restaurant that has played Middle-earth since 1972, its rooms named for hobbits and wizards, its walls thick with Tolkien lore. Long before the films, Houston had its own corner of the Shire serving sandwiches under the trees. Order a Gandalf and settle in. A taste of the Shire, on Richmond Avenue.

hook: Houston's long-standing DIY venue for experimental music, underground jazz and outsider art.
facts: Currently located at 3801 Polk Street, Houston, TX 77003, in the East End (the venue has relocated more than once over its history).
Bills itself as Houston's home for experimental electronic music, underground jazz and outsider art.
Houses the Super Happy Theater (roughly 290-capacity), an art gallery, a patio and a lending library.
Confirmed actively booking and operating as of 2026.
w: Houston's headquarters of the weird: a no-rules DIY venue for experimental music, puppet shows, noise acts and outsider art, presided over by the famous junk-collage 'Raggedy Wall.' For over twenty years it has given the unbookable a stage. Anything goes, and usually does. Step inside for whatever Houston dreamed up tonight.

hook: An immersive theatrical escape room where actors run a Houdini-style seance in real time.
facts: Located at 7026 Old Katy Road, Suite 310, Houston, TX 77024, inside the Houston Design Center.
Its flagship experience is 'The Man From Beyond: Houdini Seance,' an actor-driven immersive escape room.
Operates from a fixed venue and was confirmed open in 2026, running Thursday through Sunday.
Has been ranked among the top-rated escape rooms in the United States.
w: Less escape room than theatre: Strange Bird builds elaborate immersive experiences, including a candlelit Houdini seance where actors and illusion blur game and play. Award-winning and obsessively designed, it treats the genre as art. Solve, or just surrender to the story. Houston's most theatrical hour indoors.

hook: Houston's oldest occult and metaphysical shop, packed with ritual supplies, tarot and thousands of esoteric titles.
facts: Located at 2424 Montrose Boulevard, Houston, TX 77006, in the Montrose neighborhood.
Describes itself as the oldest and largest occult store in Houston.
Stocks metaphysical and ritual supplies including crystals, tarot, incense, herbs, and over 3,000 book titles.
Confirmed open in 2026 (Monday-Saturday 10am-7pm, Sunday 12pm-6pm).
w: For decades this Montrose shop has supplied Houston's witches, seekers and curious with herbs, candles, crystals, tarot and esoteric books. A genuine community hub, it carries the incense-and-velvet hush of a proper occult store. Browse the divination tools, or soak up the strangeness. Where the city goes for magic.

hook: Houston's 1871 hilltop garden cemetery overlooking Buffalo Bayou and the downtown skyline, final resting place of Howard Hughes.
facts: Established in 1871 as the first professionally designed cemetery in Houston; it accepted its first burial in 1872.
Laid out by landscape architect Alfred Whitaker on rolling, wooded terrain above Buffalo Bayou, breaking from the gridiron pattern of Houston's older cemeteries (garden-cemetery style).
Located at 2525 Washington Avenue, on a bluff that was a popular promenade and entertainment spot in the 1880s.
Aviator and industrialist Howard Hughes is buried in the family plot, alongside his parents Howard Hughes Sr. and Allene Gano Hughes.
Other notable interments include film star Gene Tierney, Anson Jones (last president of the Republic of Texas), Texas governors William P. Hobby and Ross S. Sterling, and heart surgeon Denton Cooley.
As of 2018 the grounds total about 84 acres, including the annexed adjacent Washington Cemetery.
w: On bluffs above Buffalo Bayou spreads Houston's loveliest cemetery, an 1871 Victorian garden of winding drives, ancient oaks and marble angels with the skyline beyond. Howard Hughes lies here, with governors, cotton kings and forgotten pioneers. Quiet, leafy and faintly Gothic. Come for the monuments and the storied dead.

hook: The 1926 Spanish Renaissance former main library, now Houston's history research center, said to be haunted by a violin-playing night watchman.
facts: Completed in 1926 as the Houston Public Library's Central Library, designed by Boston architect Ralph Adams Cram in a Spanish Renaissance Revival style.
Located at 550 McKinney Street in downtown Houston (NRHP-listed November 23, 1977).
It replaced the city's 1904 Carnegie Library and served as Houston's main library from 1926 until 1976, when the adjacent Jesse H. Jones Building opened.
Named for Julia Bedford Ideson, the Houston Public Library system's first head librarian, who led it for about 40 years.
Today it houses the Houston Metropolitan Research Center and the library's archives, manuscripts, and Texas and local history collections.
legend: Lore holds that former night watchman Jacob Frank Cramer, who died in the building in 1936, haunts it; visitors report hearing him play waltzes on a violin and the click of his German Shepherd Petey's toenails on the floor.
w: A sumptuous 1926 Spanish-Renaissance library wing of carved wood and tiled floors, now home to Houston's history archives. Staff have long reported the ghost of night watchman Jacob Cramer, who died here in 1936, and his dog — a violin heard after dark. Beautiful, and reportedly never empty.

hook: Artist live/work lofts inside the 1924 Jefferson Davis Hospital, built atop a 19th-century municipal cemetery holding thousands of graves.
facts: The building is the former Jefferson Davis Hospital, built in 1924 as the first centralized municipal hospital in Houston to treat indigent patients; it operated until 1989.
Designed by City of Houston architect W. A. Dowdy in a Classical Revival style, at 1101 Elder Street in the First Ward, northwest of downtown.

The site was Houston's municipal cemetery from about 1840 to the mid-1890s, where an estimated 6,000-plus people were buried, including Confederate soldiers, former slaves, and yellow fever and cholera victims.
After lying derelict for years, the building was renovated for about $6.3 million and reopened in 2005 as the Elder Street Artist Lofts, with 24 live/work units for artists.
It is listed on the National Register of Historic Places (Ref. No. 05000859), is a Recorded Texas Historic Landmark (2008), and was designated a City of Houston landmark on November 13, 2013.
legend: Because it sits over the old burial ground with graves reportedly still beneath the structure, residents and visitors have long traded ghost stories about the building, and it is a fixture of Houston haunted-places lore.
w: These artist lofts began as Jefferson Davis Hospital in 1924, and beneath lies a darker layer: an old pauper's and Confederate cemetery it was built over. Renovated into studios in 2005, it keeps a reputation for restless soldiers and patients. Art studios over a forgotten graveyard. Creativity, with company underfoot.

hook: The 1903 Desel-Boettcher Warehouse, long the Spaghetti Warehouse restaurant with its elevator-shaft ghost legend, now reborn as McIntyre's Downtown bar.
facts: The building at 901 Commerce Street was constructed in 1903 as the Desel-Boettcher Warehouse, used to store fur pelts and later as a pharmaceutical warehouse.
It became the Old Spaghetti Warehouse restaurant in 1974, famous for an antique trolley car, a European castle staircase, and a Penn Station chandelier in its dining room.
The restaurant flooded during Hurricane Harvey in 2017 and closed permanently; its fixtures were later auctioned off.
The building was repurposed in 2019 and now houses McIntyre's Downtown, a multi-level sports bar with a large covered open-air patio, which opened in 2021.
The historic warehouse itself remains standing in downtown's Market Square area near Buffalo Bayou.
legend: A long-told ghost story claims the building is haunted by a pharmaceutical-warehouse worker who fell to his death down an elevator shaft, along with the spirit of a woman searching for him; the elevator-shaft legend was a staple of the Spaghetti Warehouse era and downtown ghost tours.
w: A handsome 1903 brick warehouse on Commerce Street, long a Spaghetti Warehouse and home to a famous ghost story — a man who fell to his death down the elevator shaft, and the widow who soon joined him. The pasta is gone; a sports bar fills the building now. The spirits, by repute, stayed on. A haunt in both senses.

hook: The ornate 1927 Niels Esperson tower and its 1941 Mellie companion, the only complete Italian Renaissance high-rise in downtown Houston, crowned by a six-story tempietto.
facts: The 32-story Niels Esperson Building opened in 1927 at the corner of Travis and Rusk (808 Travis), designed by theater architect John Eberson; it was the tallest building in Texas when completed.
Its summit is crowned by an elaborate tiered tempietto modeled on Bramante's 1502 tempietto at San Pietro in Montorio, Rome.
Mellie Keenan Esperson, one of Houston's foremost developers of the 1920s, built the tower as a memorial to her late husband, oil pioneer Niels Esperson.
The adjoining 19-story Mellie Esperson Building was completed in 1941 in an Art Deco / Art Moderne style on the Walker Street side.
The Niels Esperson tower is described as the only complete example of Italian Renaissance architecture in downtown Houston, with massive columns, urns, and rooftop terraces.
w: Two ornate downtown towers built by an oil widow in memory of her husband: the 1927 Niels Esperson Building, crowned with an Italian-Renaissance tempietto, and its plainer 1941 partner, the Mellie. For years the taller was Houston's grandest skyscraper. Look up at the urns and rooftop folly. Gilded-age ambition, in carved stone.

hook: A roughly six-mile climate-controlled pedestrian maze running under downtown, linking some 95 city blocks one story below street level.
facts: The system is about six miles (9.7 km) of tunnels connecting roughly 95 downtown city blocks, lined with shops, restaurants and services.
It runs about 20 feet below street level and is generally open to the public on weekdays, roughly 6:00 am to 6:30 pm.
Most entry points are stairs, escalators and elevators inside private office buildings; only Wells Fargo Plaza and the McKinney Garage on Main Street offer direct street access.
The coordinate given is the Wells Fargo Plaza (1000 Louisiana Street), one of the two defensible direct public street-level access points.
The network grew piecemeal from the 1930s onward and is air-conditioned, used heavily by office workers to avoid Houston's summer heat.
w: Beneath downtown runs a hidden world: nearly six miles of climate-controlled tunnels lined with shops and lunch counters, where office workers vanish on hot afternoons. Invisible from above and closed at night, it spawns urban legends of lost workers and ghosts. Find a stairwell down. A secret city under the city.

n: Palace of the Golden Orbs (Chong Hua Sheng Mu Holy Palace)
hook: An abandoned white Taoist temple topped by a 40-foot golden orb, marooned amid west Houston strip malls, the unfinished dream of a deported sect leader.
facts: The temple, formally the Chong Hua Sheng Mu Holy Palace, stands at 3695 Overture Drive in west Houston (77082), near Beltway 8 between Synott Road and Dairy Ashford.
It was begun in 1999 by Kwai Fun Wong, leader of a Chinese Taoist organization, who bought about 11 acres intending a complex with worship, daycare, retail and residences.
Construction halted in 2001 when Wong was deported for failing to secure permanent U.S. residency, leaving the centerpiece unfinished.
Its design features a roughly 40-foot golden sphere atop a white sanctuary and two wide rooftop staircases evoking the pyramid of Chichen Itza.
The structure remains standing but abandoned and closed to the public; it was still present and listed (as closed) as of early 2026.
legend: The eerie, empty temple has become a magnet for urban explorers and local folklore, often informally called a cult temple and described in ghost-tour and oddity write-ups as one of Houston's strangest abandoned sites.
w: A gold-domed temple rises improbably from a west Houston subdivision — the unfinished Chong Hua Sheng Mu Holy Palace, begun in 1999 by a Taoist sect leader and halted when she was deported in 2001. Locals call it a 'cult temple,' and it has decayed into urban legend. View it from the street. A ruined faith, frozen half-built.

hook: A candlelit wine bar in what is reckoned Houston's oldest commercial building, the ~1860 Kennedy Bakery on Market Square.
facts: La Carafe occupies a two-story brick building at 813 Congress Street in the Market Square Historic District, widely regarded as the oldest commercial building in Houston.
The structure is the former Kennedy Bakery, built by Irishman John Kennedy; an earlier wood-framed bakery on the spot burned, and the present brick building dates to around 1860.
The original Kennedy Bakery supplied hardtack to Confederate forces during the Civil War.
By the 1950s the building had been converted into a bar, and it became La Carafe, Houston's oldest operating bar, in 1963.
It remains a candlelit tavern serving wine and beer, with no kitchen, on Market Square in downtown Houston.
legend: La Carafe is a staple of Houston ghost lore, with patrons and staff reporting an upstairs female apparition and other unexplained presences in the centuries-old building.
w: Houston's oldest bar pours wine and beer by candlelight inside what may be the city's oldest commercial building, an 1860s brick pile on Market Square, the old Kennedy Bakery. No cocktails, no kitchen, just a famous jukebox, dripping wax and old ghosts. Cash only, and all the better for it. Drink inside living history.

hook: Downtown art bar named 'Houston' spelled backward, a labyrinth of vintage shop-window displays, a mezzanine, an art gallery and an interactive 'Dome'.
facts: Located at 314 Main Street in downtown Houston, TX 77002.
Founded in 1996 as a coffee shop by artists Jim Pirtle and Missy Bosch, later becoming a bar and performance space.
Operates as an eccentric art bar and performance venue hosting experimental music, live art and community gatherings; a 21+ venue.
Listed as currently open by Downtown Houston, Visit Houston and Yelp (reviews into 2026), with regular evening hours.
w: 'Houston' spelled backward, this rambling downtown art bar has been the city's bohemian living room since 1996 — chess, poetry, jazz and oddball art upstairs. Founder Jim Pirtle made it a refuge for the strange long before downtown was cool. Order a drink and wander up. Unpretentious, ramshackle and entirely itself.

hook: A windowless octagonal interfaith chapel holding fourteen near-black Mark Rothko paintings, conceived as a space for contemplation open to all faiths.
facts: Houses fourteen murals by abstract expressionist Mark Rothko in varying hues of black, on three triptych walls and five single-painting walls.
Commissioned by John and Dominique de Menil in 1964 and completed/dedicated in 1971.
Address is 3900 Yupon Street / 1409 Sul Ross Street, Houston, TX 77006, near the Menil Collection.
A non-denominational sacred space open daily with no admission fee; reopened in 2020 after a restoration by Architecture Research Office.
w: A plain brick octagon holds fourteen vast, near-black Mark Rothko canvases, lit only from above and meant for silent contemplation by people of any faith or none. Rothko obsessed over the space but died in 1970, a year before it opened. Sit with the dark paintings and let your eyes adjust. A room built for stillness, and for grief.

hook: James Turrell light installation: a grassy pyramid topped by a knife-edge roof whose aperture frames the sky while LEDs wash the ceiling at sunrise and sunset.
facts: Erected in 2012 at the Suzanne Deal Booth Centennial Pavilion at Rice University, adjacent to Alice Pratt Brown Hall (Shepherd School of Music).
One of Turrell's largest Skyspaces and his 73rd worldwide; the first engineered for acoustics, for live and electronic performance.
Pyramidal structure of grass, concrete, stone and composite steel seating roughly 120 people across two levels, with a 72-foot square roof.
An LED light sequence projects onto the ceiling and through the roof aperture during timed sunrise and sunset viewings.
Free to visit; mapped in OpenStreetMap as 'Turrell Twilight Epiphany Skyspace' at the listed coordinate, near 6100 Main Street, Houston, TX 77005.
w: A grassy pyramid at Rice University with a knife-edged roof framing a square of open sky, where at dawn and dusk a programmed wash of coloured light plays against the changing heavens. James Turrell's Skyspace makes you watch the sky as though for the first time. Book a sunset seat and look up. Twenty minutes of slow, silent astonishment.

hook: East End Tex-Mex institution where Ninfa Laurenzo's char-grilled 'tacos al carbon' helped launch the American fajita craze.
facts: Located at 2704 Navigation Boulevard in Houston's East End.
Founded in July 1973 by Ninfa Rodriguez Laurenzo, a widow with five children, as a side business attached to her tortilla factory.
Mama Ninfa is widely credited with popularizing the fajita; her signature char-grilled beef in flour tortillas was originally called 'tacos al carbon'.
Still open as a Houston landmark; owned and operated by Legacy Restaurants since mid-2006.
w: The Tex-Mex spot that put fajitas on the American map: Mama Ninfa Laurenzo opened this East End taqueria in 1973 with her grilled tacos al carbon, and built an empire on them. The original room on Navigation still grills the beef that started it all. Order the fajitas where they were born. Houston's gift to the griddle.

hook: East Texas-style barbecue institution that grew from roadside pits in Acres Homes into a Houston smokehouse legend.
facts: Located at 8307 1/2 De Priest Street, Houston, TX 77088, in the Acres Homes area.
Roots trace to Roy Burns Sr., who sold barbecue by the roadside in Acres Homes and built it into a neighborhood institution.
The De Priest Street location was bought back, brought up to code and reopened in 2012 as Burns Original BBQ.
Currently open as the flagship; the business has since added outposts in Katy, Pearland and Summerwood.
w: A no-frills barbecue joint in Acres Homes, smoking East Texas-style brisket, ribs and links over wood for generations. Roy Burns built the name; the family carries it on, drawing crowds to a plain building far off the tourist trail. Order at the counter and eat off butcher paper. Smoke, time and serious Black Houston barbecue.

hook: Creole-spiced fried chicken landmark born in the Third Ward in 1969, a fixture between the University of Houston and Texas Southern.
facts: Founded July 3, 1969 by New Orleans native Percy 'Frenchy' Creuzot Jr. and his wife Sallie, on Scott Street in the Third Ward.
The original storefront was leased for about $2,000 and took in roughly $14 on opening day.
Sits near both the University of Houston and Texas Southern University, long a campus and neighborhood institution.
The original 3919 Scott Street site was vacated in late 2018 for the Wheeler Avenue Baptist Church expansion; after a temporary spot at 4646 Scott, a new flagship opened at 3602 Scott Street.
Founder Percy Creuzot Jr. died June 6, 2010; the restaurant remains open at the 3602 Scott Street flagship.
legend: Frenchy's is popularly said to have been a favorite of NASA and Apollo-era figures from nearby Johnson Space Center, though this association is local lore rather than documented record.
w: A Creole fried-chicken joint by the Third Ward universities, frying since 1969 to a recipe that draws a line out the door. Frenchy's built its name on crisp, spicy birds and dirty rice, feeding students, churchgoers and, so they say, the odd astronaut. The original Scott Street stand is the mothership. Soul food in the city's blood.

hook: The largest funeral museum in America, a deadpan celebration of how we send off the dead.
facts: Founded in 1992 by Robert L. Waltrip, founder of the funeral giant Service Corporation International.
Houses a large collection of historical hearses, coffins and funeral carriages.
Permanent exhibits include papal funerals, the embalming of Abraham Lincoln, and Ghanaian fantasy coffins shaped like a chicken, a fish and a Mercedes.
Located at 415 Barren Springs Drive in far-north Houston.
Its slogan is the cheerfully grim 'Any day above ground is a good one.'
w: The largest museum of death in America deals its subject with a straight face: vintage hearses, ornate coffins, the embalming of Lincoln, papal funerals and Ghanaian fantasy coffins shaped like a fish, a chicken and a Mercedes. Its slogan, only half joking: any day above ground beats the day below. Go and ponder the send-off.
